name: Alcohol (Ethanol)
summary: No known safe amount in pregnancy
detail: No amount of alcohol is known to be safe at any point in pregnancy, and exposure can cause fetal alcohol spectrum disorders. The risk applies in all three trimesters, including the earliest weeks before pregnancy is recognized. While breastfeeding, not drinking is safest; if you do have an occasional drink, waiting about 2 hours per drink before nursing limits the amount in milk.

name: Caffeine
summary: Limit to about 200 mg per day
detail: Most guidelines advise keeping caffeine below about 200 mg per day throughout pregnancy; higher intakes have been linked with miscarriage and low birth weight in some studies. Caffeine adds up across coffee, tea, cola, chocolate, and energy drinks. While breastfeeding, up to about 300 mg per day is generally considered compatible, though newborns and preterm infants clear caffeine slowly and may become irritable at higher intakes.

name: Aspartame
summary: Approved sweetener; moderation is prudent
detail: FDA and EFSA consider aspartame safe within the acceptable daily intake, and typical diet-drink consumption falls well below it. In 2023 the WHO cancer agency classified it as possibly carcinogenic at a hazard level, while the joint WHO/FAO committee kept the existing intake limit; a cautious approach is to keep intake moderate in pregnancy and while breastfeeding. People with phenylketonuria (PKU) must avoid it because it contains phenylalanine.

name: Saccharin
summary: Crosses placenta; limit during pregnancy
detail: Saccharin crosses the placenta and clears slowly from fetal tissue, so many clinicians suggest limiting it during pregnancy even though regulators permit it. Data in breastfeeding are limited; small occasional amounts are unlikely to harm, but other sweeteners have stronger safety records. Choosing products without saccharin is a reasonable conservative choice at all stages.

name: Sucralose
summary: Approved sweetener with reassuring data
detail: Sucralose is mostly not absorbed or metabolized, and reproductive studies have not shown harm at food-use levels. FDA and EFSA consider it safe in pregnancy within the acceptable daily intake. Only small amounts appear in breast milk and no infant problems have been reported; as with all sweeteners, moderation is sensible.

name: Acesulfame K
summary: Approved sweetener; fine within normal intake
detail: Acesulfame potassium is approved by FDA and EFSA, and studies have not shown reproductive harm at food-use levels. It passes into breast milk in small amounts without reported infant effects. Typical dietary exposure is well below the acceptable daily intake; heavy daily consumption of sweetened drinks is still worth moderating.

name: Stevia (Steviol Glycosides)
summary: Purified stevia considered safe in pregnancy
detail: Purified steviol glycosides are considered safe by FDA and EFSA within the acceptable daily intake, including during pregnancy. Whole-leaf stevia and crude extracts are not approved and lack safety data, so stick to purified forms found in commercial products. Breastfeeding data are limited but no problems are expected at food levels.

name: Cyclamate
summary: Banned in the US; skip when pregnant/nursing
detail: Cyclamate has been banned in the United States since 1970 over cancer concerns, although it remains permitted in some countries. Reproductive safety data are limited and it crosses the placenta. Because well-studied alternatives exist, the conservative choice is to avoid cyclamate throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Erythritol
summary: Sugar alcohol with limited pregnancy data
detail: Erythritol is a sugar alcohol generally regarded as safe, but there is little research specifically in pregnancy or breastfeeding. Research published in 2023 raised questions about cardiovascular effects at high blood levels, which regulators are still evaluating. Occasional amounts in foods are unlikely to be harmful; heavy daily intake is worth limiting until more is known.

name: Xylitol
summary: Generally safe; large amounts upset digestion
detail: Xylitol has a long history of use, including in chewing gum where it may help dental health, and no reproductive harm has been shown at food levels. Large amounts can cause gas, bloating, and laxative effects, which is a comfort issue rather than a fetal risk. Normal food and gum amounts are considered fine in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Sodium Nitrite
summary: Cured-meat preservative; limit processed meats
detail: Nitrites preserve cured meats like bacon, ham, and hot dogs and can form nitrosamines, some of which are probable carcinogens, especially with high-heat cooking. Separately, chilled ready-to-eat processed meats carry listeria risk in pregnancy unless heated until steaming hot. Occasional, well-heated servings are reasonable, but limiting overall processed-meat intake is prudent in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Sodium Nitrate
summary: Converts to nitrite; limit cured meats
detail: Nitrates used in cured meats and some cheeses convert partly to nitrite in the body, with the same nitrosamine concerns. Vegetables also naturally contain nitrates, and vegetable sources are not a concern. The practical advice is to limit cured processed meats during pregnancy and breastfeeding rather than worry about produce.

name: Sodium Benzoate
summary: Soft-drink preservative; fine occasionally
detail: Benzoates are permitted preservatives and normal dietary exposure sits within safety limits. In soft drinks that also contain vitamin C, small amounts of benzene can form, and benzoate-color mixtures were linked to hyperactivity in children in one well-known study. Occasional intake is unlikely to matter; making benzoate-preserved soft drinks a daily habit during pregnancy or breastfeeding is worth avoiding.

name: Sulfites (Sulfur Dioxide)
summary: Can trigger asthma and sensitivity reactions
detail: Sulfites preserve dried fruit, wine, and some packaged foods. They are not known to harm the fetus at permitted levels, but they can trigger wheezing and allergic-type reactions, particularly in people with asthma, which matters more when pregnant. If you have asthma or sulfite sensitivity, avoid sulfited foods; otherwise typical dietary amounts are acceptable in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole)
summary: Antioxidant flagged as possible carcinogen
detail: BHA is a synthetic antioxidant that keeps fats from going rancid. The WHO cancer agency lists it as possibly carcinogenic to humans based on high-dose animal studies, while food regulators still permit small amounts. Amounts in food are tiny, but because it is easy to avoid, minimizing regular intake during pregnancy and breastfeeding is a reasonable conservative choice.

name: BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene)
summary: Synthetic antioxidant; minimize regular intake
detail: BHT is a synthetic preservative related to BHA, used in cereals, snack foods, and packaging. High-dose animal studies have raised questions that regulators consider not relevant at the small permitted food levels. There is no direct evidence of harm in pregnancy, but limiting frequent intake of BHT-preserved foods is an easy precaution while pregnant or nursing.

name: MSG (Monosodium Glutamate)
summary: Flavor enhancer considered safe at food levels
detail: MSG is the sodium salt of glutamate, an amino acid naturally abundant in foods like tomatoes and cheese. Regulators classify it as safe, and the placenta largely metabolizes glutamate, so normal dietary MSG does not meaningfully reach the fetus. Some people report headaches or flushing after large amounts, which is a comfort issue rather than a pregnancy risk.

name: Carrageenan
summary: Thickener with debated gut-inflammation data
detail: Food-grade carrageenan, a seaweed-derived thickener in plant milks and dairy products, is permitted by FDA and EFSA. Some laboratory and animal research suggests degraded forms may promote gut inflammation, and this debate is unresolved. Typical dietary amounts are unlikely to pose a pregnancy-specific risk, but people who consume many carrageenan-containing products daily may prefer alternatives.

name: Phosphoric Acid
summary: Cola acidulant; heavy intake may affect bones
detail: Phosphoric acid gives colas their sharp taste and is safe in small amounts. Heavy, regular cola intake has been associated with lower bone mineral density, and calcium needs are higher in pregnancy and lactation. Colas also usually contain caffeine and sugar, so keeping them occasional is the practical advice at every stage.

name: Titanium Dioxide
summary: EU no longer considers this whitener safe
detail: Titanium dioxide is a white pigment used in candies, icings, and coated tablets. In 2021 EFSA concluded it could no longer be considered safe as a food additive because genotoxicity, damage to DNA, could not be ruled out, and the EU banned it in food; it remains permitted in the US. Given readily available alternatives, avoiding it during pregnancy and breastfeeding is the conservative choice.

name: Potassium Bromate
summary: Flour additive banned in much of the world
detail: Potassium bromate is a flour improver classified as possibly carcinogenic to humans and banned in the EU, UK, Canada, and many other countries, though some US flours still use it. Properly baked bread should contain little residue, but this cannot be verified from a label. Choosing bromate-free products is an easy, sensible precaution during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Brominated Vegetable Oil (BVO)
summary: Citrus-soda additive; US authorization revoked
detail: BVO kept citrus flavoring suspended in some sodas. The FDA revoked its authorization in 2024 after studies showed bromine accumulates in body fat and affects the thyroid, an organ especially important for fetal brain development. Some older or imported products may still contain it, so avoid it entirely during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Polysorbate 80
summary: Emulsifier with emerging gut-microbiome questions
detail: Polysorbate 80 is an approved emulsifier found in ice cream, sauces, and baked goods. Animal studies suggest some emulsifiers may alter the gut microbiome and promote low-grade inflammation, though human evidence is limited and regulators still permit it. Typical intakes are unlikely to be harmful, but favoring less-processed options during pregnancy and breastfeeding is a mild, reasonable precaution.

name: Carboxymethylcellulose (Cellulose Gum)
summary: Thickener with emerging gut-microbiome questions
detail: Cellulose gum is a widely used thickener that is poorly absorbed. Like polysorbate 80, some animal and small human studies suggest high intakes may alter gut bacteria, and this research is ongoing; regulators continue to permit it. There is no specific evidence of pregnancy harm, so occasional intake is fine, while heavy daily consumption of highly processed foods is worth moderating.

name: Potassium Sorbate
summary: Well-studied preservative, metabolized like fat
detail: Sorbates are among the best-tolerated preservatives; the body breaks them down like ordinary fatty acids. Long-term studies have not shown reproductive or developmental harm at food levels. No special precautions are needed during pregnancy or breastfeeding.

name: Calcium Propionate
summary: Bread mold inhibitor with a long safety record
detail: Calcium propionate prevents mold in bread and baked goods and is metabolized like naturally occurring short-chain fatty acids. Decades of use and safety reviews have not identified reproductive concerns at food levels. It is considered safe throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Tartrazine (Yellow 5)
summary: Azo dye linked to hyperactivity in children
detail: Tartrazine is an azo food dye included in the mixture linked to hyperactivity in children in the Southampton study; the EU requires a warning label on foods containing it. It can also trigger hives in a small number of sensitive people. There is no direct evidence of fetal harm at permitted levels, but it adds no nutrition and is easy to avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Sunset Yellow (Yellow 6)
summary: Azo dye; EU requires a hyperactivity warning
detail: Sunset Yellow is an azo dye that was part of the color mixtures associated with hyperactivity in children, and EU products containing it carry a warning label. Permitted levels are considered safe by regulators, and no specific fetal harm has been shown. As a purely cosmetic additive, minimizing it is a simple precaution in pregnancy and while nursing.

name: Carmoisine (Azorubine)
summary: Azo dye not permitted in the US; minimize
detail: Carmoisine is a red azo dye permitted in the EU with a hyperactivity warning label but not approved for food use in the United States. It has no nutritional value and was among the dyes studied for behavioral effects in children. Avoiding products colored with it is an easy conservative choice during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Ponceau 4R
summary: Azo dye not permitted in the US; minimize
detail: Ponceau 4R is a red azo dye linked with the Southampton hyperactivity findings and carries an EU warning label; it is not approved in the United States. Regulators consider permitted levels safe for the general population, and no specific pregnancy harm is documented. Because it is purely cosmetic, limiting it while pregnant or nursing costs nothing.

name: Allura Red (Red 40)
summary: Common azo dye; behavioral-effects debate
detail: Allura Red is the most widely used red dye in the US and is included in the group of colors debated for effects on childrens behavior; the EU requires a warning label. Reproductive studies at permitted levels have not shown fetal harm. It is safe by regulatory standards but easy to skip, which is the cautious path in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Brilliant Blue (Blue 1)
summary: Poorly absorbed dye with no pregnancy signal
detail: Brilliant Blue is very poorly absorbed from the gut and was not among the dyes linked to hyperactivity in the Southampton research. FDA and EFSA reviews have not identified reproductive or developmental concerns at food levels. Normal dietary exposure is considered safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Caramel Color (Class IV)
summary: Cola coloring with trace 4-MEI byproduct
detail: Class III and IV caramel colors, used mainly in colas and dark sodas, can contain traces of 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), a substance that caused cancer in high-dose animal studies. Regulators judge typical exposure to be low risk, and plain caramel (E150a) does not carry this concern. Keeping dark soft drinks occasional during pregnancy and breastfeeding also limits their caffeine and sugar.

name: Beta-Carotene
summary: Plant vitamin A source without retinol risks
detail: Beta-carotene is a plant pigment the body converts to vitamin A only as needed, so it does not carry the birth-defect risk of high-dose preformed retinol. As a food color and nutrient it is considered safe at all stages of pregnancy and while breastfeeding. Very high supplement doses are discouraged in smokers, but food levels are not a concern.

sources.name: NHS Vitamins and Supplements in Pregnancy

name: Annatto
summary: Natural cheese coloring; rare allergy only
detail: Annatto is a natural orange color from achiote seeds, common in cheeses and snack foods. Safety reviews have not found reproductive concerns at food levels, and it is considered safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. A small number of people have allergic reactions to it, which is unrelated to pregnancy.

name: Carmine (Cochineal)
summary: Insect-derived red color; safe, rare allergy
detail: Carmine is a natural red pigment derived from cochineal insects, used in yogurts, drinks, and candies. It has no known reproductive toxicity at food levels and is considered safe during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Rare allergic reactions, including in people with certain insect allergies, are the main documented issue.

name: Tuna
summary: Mercury varies by species; limit servings
detail: Tuna contains mercury, which can affect the developing brain, and levels vary widely by species. Canned light or skipjack tuna is a best choice at 2-3 servings per week, while albacore, white, and yellowfin should be limited to about one serving per week; bigeye tuna should be avoided. The same limits apply while breastfeeding because mercury passes into milk, and fish is otherwise a valuable source of protein and omega-3s.

name: Swordfish
summary: High-mercury fish; avoid pregnant/nursing
detail: Swordfish is one of the highest-mercury fish and is on the FDA list of choices to avoid for people who are pregnant or breastfeeding. Mercury accumulates and can harm the developing brain and nervous system in any trimester and through breast milk. Choose low-mercury fish such as salmon or sardines instead.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: King Mackerel
summary: High-mercury fish on the FDA avoid list
detail: King mackerel is a large predatory fish with high mercury levels and is on the FDA list of fish to avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Smaller mackerel species such as Atlantic mackerel are low in mercury and are good alternatives. The concern applies at all stages because mercury harms the developing nervous system.

name: Shark
summary: Very high mercury; avoid at all stages
detail: Shark (sometimes sold as flake) accumulates very high mercury levels and is listed by the FDA and NHS among fish to avoid entirely in pregnancy; the FDA avoid list also applies to breastfeeding. Mercury exposure can impair fetal and infant brain development. Other high-mercury fish to skip include marlin, tilefish from the Gulf of Mexico, and orange roughy.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Salmon (Cooked)
summary: Low-mercury fish; 2-3 servings a week encouraged
detail: Salmon is low in mercury and rich in DHA omega-3 fats that support fetal and infant brain development, so 2-3 servings per week are actively encouraged in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Cook it fully; raw or cold-smoked salmon carries separate infection risks covered under those entries. Canned salmon is an easy, safe option.

name: Sardines
summary: Low-mercury oily fish, rich in omega-3 and calcium
detail: Sardines are small, short-lived fish that accumulate very little mercury and are an FDA best choice during pregnancy and breastfeeding. They provide DHA for brain development plus calcium and vitamin D when eaten with bones. Canned sardines are cooked and safe as packaged.

name: Cold-Smoked Salmon (Lox)
summary: Listeria risk in pregnancy unless cooked hot
detail: Cold-smoked fish is not cooked and can carry listeria, which in pregnancy can cause miscarriage, stillbirth, or severe newborn infection even when the mother has mild symptoms; UK guidance now advises avoiding it in pregnancy. It becomes safe when cooked until steaming hot, for example in a fully baked dish. While breastfeeding the fetus is no longer at risk, but normal food-safety care still applies.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Raw (Unpasteurized) Milk
summary: Listeria and E. coli risk; choose pasteurized
detail: Unpasteurized milk can carry listeria, E. coli, salmonella, and campylobacter. Listeriosis in pregnancy is far more dangerous than in the general population and can cause miscarriage, stillbirth, or newborn infection in any trimester. Pasteurized milk has the same nutrition without the risk; while breastfeeding, raw milk mainly risks making the mother ill.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Unpasteurized / Soft-Ripened Cheese
summary: Soft and raw-milk cheeses carry listeria risk
detail: Soft mold-ripened cheeses (brie, camembert), soft blue cheeses, and any cheese made from unpasteurized milk carry a listeria risk and are advised against throughout pregnancy. Hard cheeses and pasteurized processed cheeses such as cheddar, mozzarella, halloumi, and cream cheese are fine, and risky cheeses become safe when cooked until steaming hot. After delivery the listeria danger to the baby largely resolves, though standard food safety still applies while nursing.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Pasteurized Milk
summary: Safe, valuable calcium and protein source
detail: Pasteurized milk is safe and beneficial throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding, providing calcium, protein, iodine, and vitamin B12. Pasteurization kills the bacteria that make raw milk risky. It is a core food for meeting increased calcium needs at every stage.

name: Raw or Undercooked Egg
summary: Salmonella risk; cook eggs or use pasteurized
detail: Raw or runny eggs can carry salmonella, and food poisoning in pregnancy can cause dehydration and, rarely, complications; it is unpleasant at any stage. Products made with pasteurized egg, including commercial mayonnaise, are fine, and in the UK, Lion-mark eggs are considered lower risk though cooking remains the conservative choice. While breastfeeding, salmonella mainly threatens the mother rather than passing through milk.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Eggs (Cooked or Pasteurized)
summary: Excellent protein and choline when fully cooked
detail: Fully cooked eggs and pasteurized egg ingredients are safe and nutritious throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding, supplying protein, choline, and vitamin D that support fetal brain development. Cook until whites and yolks are firm to remove salmonella risk. Egg ingredients in baked goods are cooked and pose no concern.

name: Unpasteurized Juice / Cider
summary: Can carry E. coli and listeria; pick pasteurized
detail: Juices that have not been pasteurized or otherwise treated can carry E. coli, salmonella, and listeria, and the FDA advises pregnant people to avoid them at all stages. Nearly all shelf-stable and most refrigerated juices are pasteurized; unpasteurized products must carry a warning label in the US. While breastfeeding the main risk is maternal illness rather than harm through milk.

name: Raw Sprouts
summary: Bacteria grow inside the seed; cook them first
detail: Raw sprouts, including alfalfa, clover, radish, and mung bean, have caused repeated salmonella and E. coli outbreaks because bacteria can live inside the seed where washing cannot reach. Health authorities advise pregnant people to avoid raw sprouts in all trimesters; thoroughly cooked sprouts are fine. After delivery the risk is ordinary food poisoning for the mother.

name: Kombucha
summary: Unpasteurized, plus trace alcohol and caffeine
detail: Most kombucha is unpasteurized, so it carries the same live-microbe risk as other raw ferments, and it also contains trace alcohol (sometimes above the labeled amount) and caffeine. Because pregnancy advice is to avoid both unpasteurized products and alcohol, skipping kombucha in all trimesters is the conservative choice. While breastfeeding, small amounts of pasteurized kombucha are likely low risk, but home-brewed versions are harder to vouch for.

sources.name: Mayo Clinic Pregnancy Week by Week

name: Liver and Liver Products
summary: Very high vitamin A can cause birth defects
detail: Liver and liver products such as pate contain very high levels of preformed vitamin A (retinol), and excess retinol can cause birth defects, with the first trimester most sensitive; NHS advice is to avoid liver products throughout pregnancy. Pate of any kind also carries a listeria risk. After delivery liver is a nutritious food again, and normal servings are fine while breastfeeding.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Honey
summary: Safe for mom; never feed directly to babies
detail: Honey is safe for pregnant and breastfeeding women; adult digestive systems handle the botulism spores that make honey dangerous for infants. The warning applies only to feeding honey directly to babies under 12 months, and botulism is not transmitted through breast milk. Treat honey as an added sugar and enjoy it in moderation.

sources.name: Mayo Clinic Pregnancy Week by Week

name: Licorice Root (Glycyrrhizin)
summary: Glycyrrhizin linked to preterm birth, worse late
detail: Real licorice contains glycyrrhizin, which can raise blood pressure and, in a well-known Finnish study, was linked to earlier delivery and effects on child cognition and stress hormones when eaten heavily in pregnancy. Concern grows in later pregnancy, so high intake should be avoided, especially in the third trimester. Most US candy uses anise flavoring rather than real licorice; while breastfeeding, occasional small amounts are likely fine but large daily intakes are discouraged.

name: Guarana
summary: Concentrated caffeine source in energy drinks
detail: Guarana is a plant with very high natural caffeine content and is common in energy drinks and some supplements. Its caffeine counts toward the roughly 200 mg daily pregnancy limit, but labels rarely quantify how much guarana contributes, making total intake hard to track. Because energy drinks often stack guarana with added caffeine and other stimulants, they are best avoided in pregnancy and limited while breastfeeding.

name: Taurine
summary: Energy-drink additive; the drinks are the concern
detail: Taurine is an amino acid found naturally in the body and in food, and moderate dietary amounts are not considered harmful. The caution is that it is concentrated in energy drinks alongside high caffeine and other stimulants, and the safety of large added doses in pregnancy has not been established. Avoiding energy drinks rather than dietary taurine is the practical advice in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Ginseng
summary: Herbal stimulant; animal data raise concern
detail: Ginseng is a stimulant herb in some teas, tonics, and energy products. A compound in ginseng caused malformations in some animal studies, and human safety data in pregnancy are inadequate, so it is generally advised against, particularly in the first trimester. It may also interact with blood-sugar and blood-thinning medicines. Given limited breastfeeding data, avoiding ginseng products is the cautious choice at all stages.

name: Ginkgo Biloba
summary: Blood-thinning herb; avoid in pregnancy/nursing
detail: Ginkgo is sold for memory and circulation and appears in some functional drinks. It can thin the blood and increase bleeding risk, a particular concern around delivery, and it lacks safety data in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Some products may also contain a natural toxin. Avoiding ginkgo at all stages is the recommended conservative approach.

name: Chamomile
summary: Popular tea with limited pregnancy safety data
detail: Chamomile tea is widely used for relaxation, and occasional cups are generally considered low risk. However, safety data in pregnancy are limited, some sources raise theoretical concerns about uterine effects with large amounts, and it may interact with blood thinners. Keeping intake occasional rather than habitual is prudent in pregnancy, and moderate use is generally regarded as compatible with breastfeeding.

name: Hibiscus
summary: May affect hormones; limit in pregnancy
detail: Hibiscus tea is tart and popular but has limited pregnancy safety data, and animal studies suggest possible hormonal and uterine effects; it may also lower blood pressure. Because of these uncertainties, avoiding it in the first trimester and limiting it later is a cautious approach. Small culinary amounts as flavoring are less concerning, and moderate intake while breastfeeding is likely low risk.

name: Black Cohosh
summary: Can stimulate the uterus; avoid entirely
detail: Black cohosh is an herb sometimes used for menopausal symptoms or to try to induce labor. It can act on hormones and stimulate uterine contractions, has been associated with rare liver injury, and lacks safety data in pregnancy and breastfeeding. It should be avoided throughout pregnancy and while nursing unless a clinician specifically directs otherwise.

name: Dong Quai
summary: Traditional herb with uterine and bleeding risks
detail: Dong quai is a traditional Chinese herb found in some tonics and teas. It is reported to have uterine-stimulating and blood-thinning effects and lacks reliable safety data in pregnancy, so it is advised against throughout. It may also increase sensitivity to sunlight and interact with anticoagulants. Avoid it during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Pennyroyal
summary: Toxic herb; can cause miscarriage and liver harm
detail: Pennyroyal is a herb historically misused to try to end pregnancy and is genuinely dangerous. Its oil is toxic to the liver and can be fatal to the mother, and it can cause miscarriage. It must be avoided completely during pregnancy and breastfeeding; there is no safe culinary or tea use.

name: Raspberry Leaf
summary: May affect the uterus; avoid early pregnancy
detail: Red raspberry leaf tea is traditionally taken in late pregnancy to prepare for labor, but it may cause uterine contractions, so it is generally advised against in the first trimester. Evidence for benefit is weak and safety data are limited, so if used at all it is typically only in later pregnancy and with a clinicians awareness. Occasional use while breastfeeding is generally considered low risk.

name: Peppermint
summary: Fine in pregnancy; large amounts may cut milk
detail: Peppermint tea and culinary peppermint are generally considered safe in pregnancy and can ease nausea, though concentrated peppermint oil supplements are a different matter. While breastfeeding, large or frequent amounts of peppermint (and its relative spearmint) are anecdotally linked to reduced milk supply in some women, so mothers with supply concerns may want to limit strong peppermint products. Occasional cups of tea are not a problem.

name: Ginger
summary: Helpful for nausea in normal food amounts
detail: Ginger is one of the better-studied remedies for pregnancy nausea and is considered safe in culinary and moderate tea amounts at all stages. Very high supplement doses are less well studied, so food and normal tea use is the sensible range. It is also considered compatible with breastfeeding.

name: Fennel
summary: Culinary use fine; avoid concentrated extracts
detail: Fennel as a vegetable or spice is fine in pregnancy, but concentrated fennel tea, extracts, and essential oil contain estragole and have possible hormonal effects, so medicinal amounts are best avoided. During breastfeeding, fennel is a traditional galactagogue, though evidence is mixed and very high intakes are discouraged. Enjoy fennel as food while being cautious with concentrated supplements.

name: Sage
summary: Cooking amounts fine; strong sage cuts milk
detail: Sage used to season food is safe, but concentrated sage tea, extracts, and oil contain thujone and large amounts are traditionally discouraged in pregnancy. While breastfeeding, sage is well known for reducing milk supply and is sometimes used deliberately during weaning, so mothers maintaining supply should avoid strong sage preparations. Normal culinary seasoning is not a concern.

name: Parsley (Concentrated)
summary: Garnish fine; large medicinal amounts risky
detail: Parsley as a garnish or normal cooking ingredient is safe, but very large medicinal doses, concentrated extracts, or parsley oil contain compounds (apiol, myristicin) that in high amounts have been linked to uterine stimulation. Practically, this means avoiding parsley supplements and juices in pregnancy rather than food-level use. Culinary parsley is fine while breastfeeding.

name: Nutmeg (Large Amounts)
summary: Baking pinch fine; large doses are psychoactive
detail: Nutmeg used as a baking spice in normal recipes is safe. Large amounts contain myristicin, which can be psychoactive and cause a racing heart, and such doses are discouraged in pregnancy. There is no need to avoid the pinch used in recipes; the caution applies to deliberately consuming large quantities. The same reasoning applies while breastfeeding.

name: Quinine (Tonic Water)
summary: Small tonic amounts likely fine; limit intake
detail: Quinine gives tonic water its bitter taste at levels far below medicinal doses. Occasional tonic water is generally considered low risk, but some people are sensitive to quinine and high medicinal doses have been associated with problems, so keeping intake modest is prudent in pregnancy. Only small amounts pass into breast milk, so moderate tonic use is compatible with breastfeeding.

name: Green Tea
summary: Caffeine plus folate-absorption concern
detail: Green tea contains caffeine that counts toward the daily limit, and matcha is more concentrated. Compounds in green tea may also interfere with folate absorption, which matters most early in pregnancy when folate protects against neural tube defects, so heavy intake is discouraged. Concentrated green tea extract supplements are a separate concern and best avoided. Moderate tea drinking is generally acceptable while breastfeeding within caffeine limits.

name: Yerba Mate
summary: Caffeinated; heavy hot intake linked to cancer
detail: Yerba mate is a caffeinated South American tea whose caffeine counts toward the pregnancy limit. Very hot and heavy long-term mate drinking has been associated with certain cancers, though this reflects extreme habitual use. Keeping intake moderate and within overall caffeine limits is the sensible approach in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Rooibos Tea
summary: Caffeine-free herbal tea, generally safe
detail: Rooibos is a naturally caffeine-free herbal tea that is widely regarded as safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding when drunk in normal amounts. It contains antioxidants and no known problematic compounds at tea-strength. As with any herbal product, keep to ordinary beverage quantities rather than concentrated extracts.

name: Aloe Vera (Whole Leaf / Latex)
summary: Oral aloe latex is a laxative; avoid ingesting
detail: Aloe latex and whole-leaf aloe taken by mouth act as a strong laxative and can stimulate the uterus, so ingesting aloe drinks and supplements is advised against in pregnancy. Purified decolorized aloe gel in some drinks is lower risk, but products are often not clearly labeled. While breastfeeding, aloe latex can pass into milk and may upset the infants stomach, so oral aloe is best avoided; topical skin use is a separate, low-concern matter.

name: Cinnamon (Large Amounts)
summary: Baking amounts fine; avoid supplement doses
detail: Cinnamon used as a spice in food is safe throughout pregnancy. Concentrated cinnamon supplements and cinnamon oil are a different matter, and large medicinal doses are not recommended; cassia cinnamon also contains coumarin, which is best limited in high amounts. There is no need to avoid cinnamon in cooking or baking. Culinary use is fine while breastfeeding.

name: Turmeric (Curcumin Supplements)
summary: Spice amounts safe; supplements less studied
detail: Turmeric used as a cooking spice or food color (E100) is considered safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. High-dose curcumin supplements are far more concentrated than food, have possible hormonal effects, and are not well studied in pregnancy, so supplement-level intake is best avoided. Enjoy turmeric in food while skipping concentrated capsules unless a clinician advises otherwise.

name: Folic Acid (Folate)
summary: Recommended; prevents neural tube defects
detail: Folic acid is actively recommended before and during pregnancy because it sharply reduces the risk of neural tube defects like spina bifida, with the first trimester most critical. A daily 400 microgram supplement is standard, and fortified foods add more. It is safe and beneficial at all stages, including breastfeeding.

sources.name: NHS Vitamins and Supplements in Pregnancy

name: Iron
summary: Needed for increased pregnancy blood volume
detail: Iron needs rise in pregnancy to support increased blood volume and the baby, and fortified foods and supplements help prevent anemia. It is safe and often recommended at all stages, though supplements can cause constipation. Very high one-time overdoses are dangerous, so keep supplements away from children, but dietary and fortified-food iron is beneficial in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Vitamin D
summary: Recommended supplement; supports bones
detail: Vitamin D supports calcium absorption and bone health for both mother and baby, and a daily 10 microgram (400 IU) supplement is recommended throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding in guidance such as the NHS. Fortified foods are a common source. It is safe at recommended doses; only extreme megadoses are a concern.

sources.name: NHS Vitamins and Supplements in Pregnancy

name: Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid)
summary: Safe nutrient and common antioxidant additive
detail: Vitamin C is essential, supports iron absorption, and is also used as the food antioxidant E300 to prevent browning and preserve freshness. Dietary and fortified amounts are safe and beneficial in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Only very high supplement megadoses are discouraged; food and additive levels are not a concern.

name: Vitamin A (Retinol / Retinyl)
summary: High preformed vitamin A can cause birth defects
detail: Preformed vitamin A (retinol and retinyl esters), as opposed to plant beta-carotene, can cause birth defects at high doses, with the first trimester most sensitive; supplements high in retinol and vitamin-A-fortified products in large amounts should be avoided. Small amounts in a balanced diet and prenatal vitamins formulated for pregnancy are fine. Vitamin A needs are actually higher during breastfeeding, so normal intake is beneficial then.

sources.name: NHS Vitamins and Supplements in Pregnancy

name: Calcium Carbonate
summary: Calcium source and antacid; safe and useful
detail: Calcium carbonate is used to fortify foods, as the additive E170, and as an antacid for heartburn, which is common in pregnancy. Calcium needs are high in pregnancy and lactation, so it is both safe and helpful. Keep total calcium within recommended limits, but ordinary food, fortification, and occasional antacid use are fine at all stages.

name: Iodine
summary: Essential for fetal brain and thyroid
detail: Iodine is essential for thyroid hormones that drive fetal brain development, and needs increase in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Iodized salt, dairy, and seafood are key sources, and mild deficiency is common. Recommended amounts are safe and important; only very high excess intakes, such as from certain seaweed supplements, should be avoided.

name: Yogurt Cultures / Probiotics
summary: Live yogurt cultures are safe and beneficial
detail: The live bacterial cultures in yogurt and many probiotic foods are considered safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding and provide calcium and protein when made from pasteurized milk. Pasteurized yogurt is safe even though it contains live cultures, because the milk itself was pasteurized before culturing. Probiotics have a good safety record for healthy pregnant people.

name: Pectin
summary: Natural fruit gelling fiber; safe
detail: Pectin is a natural fiber from fruit used to set jams and thicken foods (E440). It is not absorbed and has an excellent safety record, with no reproductive concerns. It is safe throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Citric Acid
summary: Ubiquitous, well-tolerated acidity regulator
detail: Citric acid occurs naturally in citrus fruit and is one of the most common food additives (E330), used for tartness and as a preservative. It is metabolized normally by the body and has no known reproductive risks. It is safe at all stages of pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Lecithin
summary: Common emulsifier; safe, provides choline
detail: Lecithin (E322), usually from soy or sunflower, is a natural emulsifier in chocolate, spreads, and baked goods and is a source of choline, which supports fetal brain development. It has a long, reassuring safety record. It is safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding, and is even sometimes used to help prevent blocked milk ducts.

name: Xanthan Gum
summary: Common thickener; safe, not absorbed
detail: Xanthan gum (E415) is a fermentation-derived thickener widely used in gluten-free and sauce products. It passes through the gut largely undigested and has an excellent safety profile with no reproductive concerns. It is safe throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Guar Gum
summary: Plant-fiber thickener; safe in food amounts
detail: Guar gum (E412) is a soluble fiber from guar beans used to thicken and stabilize foods. In food amounts it is safe and well tolerated, though very large supplement doses can cause bloating. There are no reproductive concerns, and it is safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Sodium Alginate
summary: Seaweed-derived thickener; safe
detail: Alginates (E400-E405) are gelling and thickening agents extracted from brown seaweed, used in dairy, sauces, and some medicines. They are not absorbed and have a strong safety record with no reproductive concerns. They are considered safe throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Baking Soda (Sodium Bicarbonate)
summary: Leavening agent; safe in baking
detail: Sodium bicarbonate (E500) is a leavening agent in baked goods and is safe in food amounts during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Used occasionally as an antacid it is also generally fine, though its high sodium content means it should not be taken in large medicinal doses without advice. As a baking ingredient it poses no concern.

name: Yeast
summary: Baking and nutritional yeast are safe
detail: Baking yeast, nutritional yeast, and yeast extract are safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding, and nutritional yeast can add B vitamins. Baking fully kills the yeast, and nutritional yeast is deactivated. Yeast extract also contributes savory flavor; only its salt content is worth keeping in perspective.

name: Vinegar
summary: Culinary vinegar is safe; pick pasteurized
detail: Vinegar and its acetic acid (E260) are safe as culinary ingredients in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Pasteurized vinegars are best; raw unfiltered vinegars with the mother are generally low risk but less controlled. Any residual alcohol in vinegar is negligible and not a concern.

name: Olive Oil
summary: Healthy fat; encouraged in pregnancy
detail: Olive oil is a heart-healthy unsaturated fat and a staple of the Mediterranean diet that is encouraged during pregnancy and breastfeeding. It has no safety concerns and provides vitamin E and beneficial fats. It is a good choice for cooking and dressings at every stage.

name: Whole Grains
summary: Fiber, B vitamins; encouraged at all stages
detail: Whole grains such as oats, brown rice, whole wheat, and quinoa provide fiber, B vitamins, and iron, and are encouraged throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding. Fiber also helps with the constipation that is common in pregnancy. They are a foundation of a healthy diet at every stage.

name: Omega-3 (DHA / EPA)
summary: Supports fetal brain and eye development
detail: Omega-3 fats, especially DHA, support fetal brain and eye development and are encouraged in pregnancy and breastfeeding, whether from low-mercury fish or algal-oil supplements. Fish-derived oils should be from body oil rather than liver oil to avoid excess vitamin A; cod liver oil is discouraged for that reason. Algal DHA is a good vegetarian source.

name: Gelatin
summary: Animal-derived gelling protein; safe
detail: Gelatin is a protein from animal collagen used to set desserts and gummies and is safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. It is processed and carries no infection or reproductive concerns as sold. There are no special precautions beyond ordinary dietary balance.

name: Agar
summary: Seaweed gelling agent; safe
detail: Agar (E406) is a plant-based gelling agent from seaweed, used as a vegetarian alternative to gelatin. It is not absorbed and has no reproductive concerns, making it safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Very large amounts act as a mild laxative, which is a comfort issue only.

name: Maltodextrin
summary: Common starch-based carbohydrate; safe
detail: Maltodextrin is a starch-derived carbohydrate used as a filler and thickener in many packaged foods. It is digested like other carbohydrates and has no reproductive concerns. It is safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding, though as a rapidly absorbed carbohydrate it is worth accounting for in blood-sugar management.

name: Mono- and Diglycerides
aliases: mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids
summary: Fat-based emulsifier; safe at food levels
detail: Mono- and diglycerides (E471) are fat-derived emulsifiers used in bread, margarine, and ice cream. The body digests them like ordinary dietary fat, and they have a long safety record with no reproductive concerns. They are considered safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Deli Meat / Cold Cuts
summary: Listeria risk unless heated until steaming
detail: Ready-to-eat deli meats can carry listeria, which is far more dangerous in pregnancy and can cause miscarriage, stillbirth, or newborn infection in any trimester. Heating cold cuts until steaming hot (about 74C) just before eating removes the risk. They also count as processed meats high in nitrites and salt. After delivery the listeria danger to the baby resolves, so cold cuts are fine while breastfeeding.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Monk Fruit Extract
summary: Plant sweetener generally recognized as safe
detail: Monk fruit extract is a natural high-intensity sweetener that the FDA lists as generally recognized as safe. It is poorly absorbed and has no known reproductive concerns, so it is considered safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Dedicated pregnancy studies are limited, but its safety profile is reassuring at food levels.

name: Sorbitol
summary: Sugar alcohol; large amounts loosen stools
detail: Sorbitol (E420) is a sugar alcohol used in sugar-free gum and candies. It is safe at food levels in pregnancy and breastfeeding, with no reproductive concerns. Larger amounts have a laxative effect and can cause gas and bloating, which is a comfort issue rather than a risk.

name: High-Fructose Corn Syrup
summary: Added sugar; safe but best limited
detail: High-fructose corn syrup is a common added sugar in sodas and packaged foods. It is not toxic and has no specific reproductive risk, so it is technically safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. However, like all added sugars it is worth limiting to help manage weight gain and blood-sugar, which matters more with the risk of gestational diabetes.

name: Seaweed / Kelp
summary: Iodine overload risk; hijiki has arsenic
detail: Small amounts of seaweed such as nori are fine, but some seaweeds, especially kelp and kombu, are extremely high in iodine, and too much iodine can disturb the fetal thyroid just as too little can. Hijiki additionally can contain high inorganic arsenic and is best avoided. Enjoy modest amounts of low-iodine seaweed, but avoid concentrated kelp supplements in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Cocoa / Chocolate
summary: Fine in moderation; contains some caffeine
detail: Chocolate is safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding in normal amounts and is a nice treat, but it contains caffeine and the related stimulant theobromine, which count toward daily caffeine limits, especially with dark chocolate. Large quantities of dark chocolate can add up, so keep it within your overall caffeine budget. Occasional chocolate has no meaningful effect on a breastfed baby.

name: Energy Drinks
summary: High caffeine stacked with untested stimulants
detail: Energy drinks combine high caffeine with guarana, taurine, ginseng, and other additives whose combined effects are not well studied in pregnancy, and they can easily push caffeine past safe limits. Health bodies advise pregnant people to avoid them. While breastfeeding they are best limited, both for caffeine reaching the baby and the uncertain herbal stimulants; water and food are better sources of energy.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy

name: Tofu / Tempeh (Soy Protein)
summary: Nutritious protein; whole-soy foods are safe
detail: Whole soy foods such as tofu, tempeh, and edamame are good sources of protein and are safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding as part of a varied diet. Concern about soy is mainly theoretical and tied to very high-dose isoflavone supplements rather than ordinary soy foods. Cook tempeh and pasteurized soy products as usual; there is no need to avoid dietary soy.

name: Modified Food Starch
summary: Processed starch thickener; safe
detail: Modified food starch is ordinary starch treated to improve its thickening and stability, used across many packaged foods. It is digested like other starch and has no reproductive concerns. It is safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Salt (Sodium Chloride)
summary: Needed, but keep intake moderate
detail: Salt is a normal dietary need and does not need to be avoided in pregnancy; iodized salt also helps supply iodine. Very high sodium intake is worth moderating, particularly with high blood pressure or swelling, which can matter in later pregnancy. Overall, ordinary seasoning is safe at all stages, with balance the main goal.

name: Raw Shellfish / Oysters
summary: Raw shellfish carries vibrio and norovirus risk
detail: Raw and undercooked shellfish can carry vibrio bacteria, norovirus, and other pathogens that cause severe food poisoning, which is riskier in pregnancy because of a weakened immune system and the danger of dehydration. Cooked shellfish is safe and low in mercury, so the advice is to cook it thoroughly rather than avoid shellfish altogether. While breastfeeding the concern is mainly maternal illness rather than harm through milk.

sources.name: NHS Foods to Avoid in Pregnancy
